question_text: Of the students in a college, it is known that 60% reside in hostel and 40% are day scholars (not residing in hostel). Previous year results report that 30% of all students who reside in hostel attain. A grade and 20% of day scholars attain A grade in their annual examination. At the end of year, one student is chosen at random from the college and he has an A grade, what is the probability that the student is a hostlier?
question_html: Of the students in a college, it is known that 60% reside in hostel and 40% are day scholars (not residing in hostel). Previous year results report that 30% of all students who reside in hostel attain. A grade and 20% of day scholars attain A grade in their annual examination. At the end of year, one student is chosen at random from the college and he has an A grade, what is the probability that the student is a hostlier?
solution_text: Let us define the events as E 1 : Student reside in a hostel. E 2 : Student is a day scholar. A: Student gets A grade. Then, P(E 1 ) = Probability that student resides in a hostel = 60% = {tex}\frac { 60 } { 100 }{/tex} and P(E 2 ) = Probability that student is day scholar {tex}= 1 - \frac { 60 } { 100 } = \frac { 40 } { 100 }{/tex} Also, P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = Probability that the student residing in a hostel gets A grade = 30% = {tex} \frac { 30 } { 100 }{/tex} and P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_2}{/tex} ) = Probability that student having day scholars gets A grade = 20% = {tex} \frac { 20 } { 100 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The probability that the selected student is a hosteler having A grade = {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{ A}) = \frac{{P({E_1}).P(A/{E_1})}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A/{E_1}} \right) + P({E_2}).P(A/{E_2})}}{/tex} [by Bayes's therorem] {tex}= \frac { \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 30 } { 100 } } { \left( \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 30 } { 100 } \right) + \left( \frac { 40 } { 100 } \times \frac { 20 } { 100 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1800 } { 1800 + 800 } = \frac { 1800 } { 2600 } = \frac { 18 } { 26 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us define the events as
E1: Student reside in a hostel.
E2: Student is a day scholar.
A: Student gets  A grade.
Then,
P(E1) = Probability that student resides in a hostel
= 60% = {tex}\frac { 60 } { 100 }{/tex}
and P(E2) = Probability that student is 
day scholar{tex}= 1 - \frac { 60 } { 100 } = \frac { 40 } { 100 }{/tex}
Also, P({tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex}) = Probability that the student residing in a hostel gets A grade = 30% = {tex} \frac { 30 } { 100 }{/tex}
and P({tex}\frac {A}{E_2}{/tex}) = Probability that student having
day scholars gets A grade = 20% = {tex} \frac { 20 } { 100 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The probability that the selected student is a hosteler having A grade
= {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{ A}) = \frac{{P({E_1}).P(A/{E_1})}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A/{E_1}} \right) + P({E_2}).P(A/{E_2})}}{/tex}
[by Bayes's therorem]
{tex}= \frac { \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 30 } { 100 } } { \left( \frac { 60 } { 100 } \times \frac { 30 } { 100 } \right) + \left( \frac { 40 } { 100 } \times \frac { 20 } { 100 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1800 } { 1800 + 800 } = \frac { 1800 } { 2600 } = \frac { 18 } { 26 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 }{/tex}

question_html: In answering a question on a multiple choice test, a student either knows the answer or guesses. Let {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} be the probability that he knows the answer and {tex}\frac {1}{4}{/tex} be the probability that he guesses. Assuming that a student who guesses at the answer will be correct with probability {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}. What is the probability that the student knows the answer given that he answered it correctly?
solution_text: Let E 1 : the student knows the answer, then, P(E 1 ) = {tex} \frac{3}{4}{/tex} E 2 : the student guesses the answer, then, P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Let A: the answer is correct. {tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) {/tex} = 1, {tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{1}{4}{/tex} Hence, by Baye's theorm, we have, {tex}P(\frac {E_1}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{1 \times \frac{3}{4}}}{{1 \times \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{{12}}{{13}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 : the student knows the answer, then, P(E1) = {tex} \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
E2 : the student guesses the answer, then, P(E2) = {tex} \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Let A: the answer is correct.
{tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) {/tex} = 1, {tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
Hence, by Baye's theorm, we have,
{tex}P(\frac {E_1}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{1 \times \frac{3}{4}}}{{1 \times \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4} \times \frac{1}{4}}} = \frac{{12}}{{13}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A laboratory blood test is 99% effective in detecting a certain disease when it is in fact, present. However, the test also yields a false positive result for 0.5% of the healthy person tested (i. e if a healthy person is tested, then, with probability 0.005, the test will imply he has the disease). If 0.1 percent of the population actually has the disease, what is the probability that a person has the disease given that his test result is positive?
question_html: A laboratory blood test is 99% effective in detecting a certain disease when it is in fact, present. However, the test also yields a false positive result for 0.5% of the healthy person tested (i. e if a healthy person is tested, then, with probability 0.005, the test will imply he has the disease). If 0.1 percent of the population actually has the disease, what is the probability that a person has the disease given that his test result is positive?
solution_text: E 1 : the person has the disease E 2 : the person is healthy A : test is positive P(E 1 ) = 0.1 = {tex} \frac{1}{{10}}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}1 - \frac{1}{{10}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex} P( {tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{{99}}{{100}}{/tex} , {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.005 {tex} = \frac{5}{{1000}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{{99}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{10}}}}{{\frac{{99}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{10}} + \frac{5}{{1000}} + \frac{9}{{10}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{22}}{{23}}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : the person has the disease
E2 : the person is healthy
A : test is positive
P(E1) = 0.1 = {tex} \frac{1}{{10}}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}1 - \frac{1}{{10}} = \frac{9}{{10}}{/tex}
P({tex}\frac {A}{E_1}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{{99}}{{100}}{/tex}, {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.005
{tex} = \frac{5}{{1000}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{{99}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{10}}}}{{\frac{{99}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{10}} + \frac{5}{{1000}} + \frac{9}{{10}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{22}}{{23}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: There are three coins. One is a two headed coin (having head on both faces), another is a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the time and third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows heads, what is the probability that it was the two headed coin?
question_html: There are three coins. One is a two headed coin (having head on both faces), another is a biased coin that comes up heads 75% of the time and third is an unbiased coin. One of the three coins is chosen at random and tossed, it shows heads, what is the probability that it was the two headed coin?
solution_text: Let E 1 : Two headed coin, E 2 : Biased coin and E 3 : Unbiased coin. Let A : Coin shows head. Then P (E 1 ) = P (E 2 ) = P (E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} As the two-headed coin has head on both sides so it will show head only. Also P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex} ) = P (correct answer given that he knows) = 1 And P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex} ) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is biased) = 75% = {tex}\frac{75}{100}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} And P( {tex}\frac{A}{E_3}{/tex} ) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is unbiased) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now the probability that the coin is two headed, being given that it shows head, is P( {tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex} ). By using Bayes’ theorem, we have: {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}\left(1+\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{9}{4}}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} P( {tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex} ) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 : Two headed coin,
E2 : Biased coin
and E3 : Unbiased coin.
Let A : Coin shows head.
Then P (E1) = P (E2) = P (E3) = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
As the two-headed coin has head on both sides so it will show head only.
Also P({tex}\frac{A}{E_1}{/tex}) = P (correct answer given that he knows) = 1
And P({tex}\frac{A}{E_2}{/tex}) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is biased) = 75% = {tex}\frac{75}{100}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} 
And P({tex}\frac{A}{E_3}{/tex}) = P (coin shows head given that the coin is unbiased) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Now the probability that the coin is two headed, being given that it shows head, is P({tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex}).
By using Bayes’ theorem, we have:
{tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{A}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{3}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{A} | \mathrm{E}_{3}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1}{\frac{1}{3} \cdot 1+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}\left(1+\frac{3}{4}+\frac{1}{2}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{9}}{\frac{9}{4}}=\frac{4}{9}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P({tex}\frac{E_1}{A}{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{4}{9}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: An insurance company insured 2000 scooter drivers, 4000 car drivers and 6000 truck drivers. The probability of an accidents are 0.01, 0.03 and 0.15 respectively. One of the insured persons meet with an accident. What is the probability that he is a scooter driver?
question_html: An insurance company insured 2000 scooter drivers, 4000 car drivers and 6000 truck drivers. The probability of an accidents are 0.01, 0.03 and 0.15 respectively. One of the insured persons meet with an accident. What is the probability that he is a scooter driver?
solution_text: E 1 : Insured person is a scooter driver E 2 : Insured person is a car driver E 3 : Insured person is a truck driver P(E 1 ) = {tex} \frac{{2000}}{{2000 + 4000 + 6000}} = \frac{{2000}}{{12000}} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex} P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac{1}{3}{/tex} P(E 3 ) = {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let A insured per son meets with an accident {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.01 = {tex} \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.03 = {tex} \frac{3}{{100}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_3}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.15 = {tex} \frac{{15}}{{100}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{E_1}{{{A}}}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) + P({E_3})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_3}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{{100}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{15}}{{100}}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{52}}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : Insured person is a scooter driver
E2 : Insured person is a car driver
E3 : Insured person is a truck driver
P(E1) = {tex} \frac{{2000}}{{2000 + 4000 + 6000}} = \frac{{2000}}{{12000}} = \frac{1}{6}{/tex}
P(E2) = {tex} \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
P(E3) = {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Let A insured per son meets with an accident
{tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.01 = {tex} \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.03 = {tex} \frac{3}{{100}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_3}}}} \right) {/tex} = 0.15 = {tex} \frac{{15}}{{100}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{E_1}{{{A}}}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) + P({E_3})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_3}}}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{3}{{100}} + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{{15}}{{100}}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{{52}}{/tex}

question_text: A factory has two machines A and B. Past record shows that machine A produced 60% of the items of output and machine B produced 40% of the items. Further, 2% of the items produced by machine A and 1% produced by machine B were defective. All the items are put into one stockpile and then one item is chosen at random from this and is found to be defective. What is the probability that it was produced by machine B?
question_html: A factory has two machines A and B. Past record shows that machine A produced 60% of the items of output and machine B produced 40% of the items. Further, 2% of the items produced by machine A and 1% produced by machine B were defective. All the items are put into one stockpile and then one item is chosen at random from this and is found to be defective. What is the probability that it was produced by machine B?
solution_text: Given: P(A) = {tex}\frac{{60}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(B) = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{100}}{/tex} Let D denotes a defective item : {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(D|A) = {tex}\frac{2}{{100}}{/tex} and P(D|B) {tex} = \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} P(B|D) = {tex}\frac{{P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{{P\left( A \right)P\left( {D|A} \right) + P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{60}}{{100}} \times \frac{2}{{100}} + \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{120 + 40}} = \frac{{40}}{{160}} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: P(A) = {tex}\frac{{60}}{{100}}{/tex},  P(B) = {tex}\frac{{40}}{{100}}{/tex}
Let D denotes a defective item :
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P(D|A) = {tex}\frac{2}{{100}}{/tex}and P(D|B) {tex} = \frac{1}{{100}}{/tex}
P(B|D) ={tex}\frac{{P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{{P\left( A \right)P\left( {D|A} \right) + P\left( B \right)P\left( {D|B} \right)}}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{{\frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{60}}{{100}} \times \frac{2}{{100}} + \frac{{40}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{{40}}{{120 + 40}} = \frac{{40}}{{160}} = \frac{1}{4}{/tex}

question_text: Two groups are competing for the position on the Board of directors of a corporation. The probabilities that the first and the second groups will win are 0.6 and 0.4 respectively. Further, if the first group wins, the probability of introducing a new product is 0.7 and the corresponding probability if 0.3, if the second group wins. Find the probability that the new product introduced was by the second group.
question_html: Two groups are competing for the position on the Board of directors of a corporation. The probabilities that the first and the second groups will win are 0.6 and 0.4 respectively. Further, if the first group wins, the probability of introducing a new product is 0.7 and the corresponding probability if 0.3, if the second group wins. Find the probability that the new product introduced was by the second group.
solution_text: Given: P (G 1 ) = 0.6, P (G 2 ) = 0.4 Let P denotes the launching of new product. {tex}\therefore {/tex} P(P | G 1 ) = 0.7, P(P | G 2 ) = 0.3 P(G 1 |P) = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{G_1}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_1}} \right) + P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{0.4 \times 0.3}}{{0.6 \times 0.7 + 0.4 \times 0.3}} = \frac{{12}}{{54}} = \frac{2}{9}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: P (G1) = 0.6, P (G2) = 0.4
Let P denotes the launching of new product.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} P(P|G1) = 0.7, P(P|G2) = 0.3
P(G1|P) ={tex}\frac{{P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{G_1}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_1}} \right) + P\left( {{G_2}} \right)P\left( {P|{G_2}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{0.4 \times 0.3}}{{0.6 \times 0.7 + 0.4 \times 0.3}} = \frac{{12}}{{54}} = \frac{2}{9}{/tex}

question_text: Suppose a girl throws a die. If she gets a 5 or 6, she tosses a coin three times and notes the number of heads. If she gets 1, 2, 3 or 4, she tosses a coin once and notes whether a head or tail is obtained. If she obtained exactly one head, what is the probability that she threw 1, 2, 3 or 4 with the die?
question_html: Suppose a girl throws a die. If she gets a 5 or 6, she tosses a coin three times and notes the number of heads. If she gets 1, 2, 3 or 4, she tosses a coin once and notes whether a head or tail is obtained. If she obtained exactly one head, what is the probability that she threw 1, 2, 3 or 4 with the die?
solution_text: E 1 : 1, 2, 3, 4 is shown on dice E 2 : 5 or 6 is shown on dice P(E 1 ) = {tex} \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex} Let, A exactly one head shown up {tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) = \frac{1}{2},P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{3}{8}{/tex} {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}} = \frac{8}{{11}}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : 1, 2, 3, 4 is shown on dice
E2 : 5 or 6 is shown on dice
P(E1) = {tex} \frac{4}{6} = \frac{2}{3}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex} \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
Let, A exactly one head shown up
{tex}P(\frac {A}{{E_1}}) = \frac{1}{2},P(\frac {A}{{E_2}}) = \frac{3}{8}{/tex}
{tex}P(\frac {{E_1}}{A}) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P(A/{E_1}) + P({E_2})P(A/{E_2})}} = \frac{8}{{11}}{/tex}

question_text: A manufacturer has three machine operators A, B and C. The first operator A produces 1% defective items, where as the other two operators B and C produce 5% and 7% defective items respectively. A is on the job for 50% of the time, B is on the job for 30% of the time and C on the job for 20% of the time. A defective item is produced, what is the probability that it was produced by A?
question_html: A manufacturer has three machine operators A, B and C. The first operator A produces 1% defective items, where as the other two operators B and C produce 5% and 7% defective items respectively. A is on the job for 50% of the time, B is on the job for 30% of the time and C on the job for 20% of the time. A defective item is produced, what is the probability that it was produced by A?
solution_text: Let E 1 = the item is manufactured by the operator A, E 2 = the item is manufactured by the operator B, E 3 = the item is manufactured by the operator C and A = the item is defective Now P (E 1 ) = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{{30}}{{100}}{/tex} , P(E 3 ) = {tex}\frac{{20}}{{100}}{/tex} Now {tex}P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex} = P(item drawn is manufactured by operator A) = {tex}\frac{1}{{100}}{/tex} Similarly, {tex}P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{{100}}{/tex} Now Required probability = Probability that the item is manufactured by operator. A given that the item drawn is defective {tex}P(\frac {{E_1}} {A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{{30}}{{100}} \times \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{7}{{100}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{50 + 150 + 140}} = \frac{5}{{34}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let E1 = the item is manufactured by the operator A, E2 = the item is manufactured by the operator B, E3 = the item is manufactured by the operator C and A = the item is defective
Now P (E1) = {tex}\frac{{50}}{{100}}{/tex}, P(E2) = {tex}\frac{{30}}{{100}}{/tex}, P(E3) = {tex}\frac{{20}}{{100}}{/tex}
Now {tex}P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex} = P(item drawn is manufactured by operator A) = {tex}\frac{1}{{100}}{/tex}
Similarly, {tex}P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{5}{{100}}{/tex} and {tex}P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{7}{{100}}{/tex}
Now Required probability = Probability that the item is manufactured by operator. A given that the item drawn is defective
{tex}P(\frac {{E_1}} {A}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right)}}{{P\left( {{E_1}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P\left( {{E_2}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) + P\left( {{E_3}} \right)P\left( {A|{E_3}} \right)}}{/tex}

= {tex}\frac{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}}}}{{\frac{{50}}{{100}} \times \frac{1}{{100}} + \frac{{30}}{{100}} \times \frac{5}{{100}} \times \frac{7}{{100}}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{{50}}{{50 + 150 + 140}} = \frac{5}{{34}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn and are found to be both diamonds. Find the probability of the lost card being a diamond.
question_html: A card from a pack of 52 cards is lost. From the remaining cards of the pack, two cards are drawn and are found to be both diamonds. Find the probability of the lost card being a diamond.
solution_text: E 1 : lost card is diamond E 2 : lost card is not diamond let A: two cards drawn from the remaining pack are diamonds. {tex}P({E_1}) = \frac{{13}}{{52}} = \frac{1}{4},P({E_2}) = \frac{{39}}{{52}} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{{12{C_2}}}{{51{C_2}}} = \frac{{12 \times 11}}{{51 \times 50}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{{13{C_2}}}{{51{C_2}}} = \frac{{13 \times 12}}{{51 \times 50}}{/tex} {tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{13}{52}×\frac{12×11}{51×50}}{\frac{13}{52}×\frac{12×11}{51×50}+\frac{3}{4}×\frac{13×12}{51×50}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{11}}{{50}}{/tex}
solution_html:
E1 : lost card is diamond
E2 : lost card is not diamond
let A: two cards drawn from the remaining pack are diamonds.
{tex}P({E_1}) = \frac{{13}}{{52}} = \frac{1}{4},P({E_2}) = \frac{{39}}{{52}} = \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) = \frac{{12{C_2}}}{{51{C_2}}} = \frac{{12 \times 11}}{{51 \times 50}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right) = \frac{{13{C_2}}}{{51{C_2}}} = \frac{{13 \times 12}}{{51 \times 50}}{/tex}
{tex}P\left( {\frac{{{E_1}}}{A}} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right)}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_1}}}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {\frac{A}{{{E_2}}}} \right)}}{/tex}

 {tex}=\frac{\frac{13}{52}×\frac{12×11}{51×50}}{\frac{13}{52}×\frac{12×11}{51×50}+\frac{3}{4}×\frac{13×12}{51×50}}{/tex}

question_text: Probability that A speaks truth is {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex} . A coin is tossed. A reports that a head appears. The probability that actually there was head is
question_html: Probability that A speaks truth is {tex}\frac{4}{5}{/tex}. A coin is tossed. A reports that a head appears. The probability that actually there was head is

options.explanation: Let E 1 be the event that A speaks the truth, E 2 be the event that A lies and X be the event that it appears head. Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right)=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} E 1 and E 2 are events that are complementary to each other. Then P (E 1 ) + P (E 2 ) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P (E 2 ) = 1 - P (E 1 ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P (E 2 ) = {tex}1-\frac{4}{5}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} If a coin is tossed it may show head or tail. Hence the probability of getting a head is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} , irrespective of whether A speaks truth or lies. i,e, P(X|E 1 ) = P(X|E 2 ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now the probability that actually there was head, given that A speaks truth is P(E 1 |X). By using bayes’ theorem, we have: {tex}\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{x}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{X} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)}{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{X} | \mathrm{E}_{1}\right)+\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{2}\right) \cdot \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{X} | \mathrm{E}_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{\frac{4}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{1}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{2}{5}}{\frac{2}{5}+\frac{1}{10}}=\frac{\frac{2}{5}}{\frac{5}{10}}=\frac{4}{5}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{E}_{1} | \mathrm{x}\right)=\frac{4}{5}{/tex}

question_text: A and B are two events such that P (A) {tex}\ne{/tex} 0. Find P(B|A), if A is a subset of B.
question_html: A and B are two events such that P (A) {tex}\ne{/tex} 0. Find P(B|A), if A is a subset of B.
solution_text: Given two events A and B where P(A) {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 and {tex}A \subset B{/tex} We have, {tex}A \cap B=A{/tex} {tex}\therefore P(A \cap B)=P(B \cap A)=P(A){/tex} Hence, {tex}P(B | A)=\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{P(A)}{P(A)}{/tex} = 1

Hence, {tex}P(B | A)=\frac{P(B \cap A)}{P(A)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{P(A)}{P(A)}{/tex} 
= 1

solution_text: Given that, a couple has two children. B : At least one of the children is male. Thus, we have; A {tex}\cap{/tex} B = {(b, b)} P(A {tex}\cap{/tex} B) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P(A) = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} P(B) = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex} Hence, {tex}P(A | B)=\frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{4}}{\frac{3}{4}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_text: If a leap year is selected at random, what is the chance that it will contain 53 tuesdays?
question_html: If a leap year is selected at random, what is the chance that it will contain 53 tuesdays?
solution_text: We know that, in a leap year there are total 366 days, 52 weeks and 2 days. Now, in 52 weeks there are total 52 Tuesdays. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Probability that the leap year will contain 53 Tuesdays is equal to the probability of remaining 2 days will be Tuesdays. Thus, the remaining two days can be: (Monday and Tuesday), (Tuesday and Wednesday), (Wednesday and Thursday), (Thursday and Friday), (Friday and Saturday), (Saturday and Sunday) and (Sunday and Monday) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Number of cases = 7 Cases in which Tuesday can come = 2 Hence, probability (leap year having 53 Tuesdays) = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, in a leap year there are total 366 days, 52 weeks and 2 days.
Now, in 52 weeks there are total 52 Tuesdays.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Probability that the leap year will contain 53 Tuesdays is equal to the probability of remaining 2 days will be Tuesdays.
Thus, the remaining two days can be:
(Monday and Tuesday), (Tuesday and Wednesday), (Wednesday and Thursday), (Thursday and Friday), (Friday and Saturday), (Saturday and Sunday) and (Sunday and Monday)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Number of cases = 7
Cases in which Tuesday can come = 2
Hence, probability (leap year having 53 Tuesdays) = {tex}\frac{2}{7}{/tex}

question_text: Assume that the chances of a patient having a heart attack is 40%. It is also assumed that a meditation and yoga course reduce the risk of heart attack by 30% and prescription of certain drug reduces its chances by 25%. At a time a patient can choose any one of the two options with equal probabilities. It is given that after going through one of the two options the patient selected at random suffers a heart attack. Find the probability that the patient followed a course of meditation and yoga?
question_html: Assume that the chances of a patient having a heart attack is 40%. It is also assumed that a meditation and yoga course reduce the risk of heart attack by 30% and prescription of certain drug reduces its chances by 25%. At a time a patient can choose any one of the two options with equal probabilities. It is given that after going through one of the two options the patient selected at random suffers a heart attack. Find the probability that the patient followed a course of meditation and yoga?
solution_text: A patient has options to have the treatment of yoga and meditation and that of prescription of drugs. Let these events be denoted by E 1 and E 2 i.e., E 1 = Treatment of yoga and meditation E 2 = Treatment of prescription of certain drugs P (E 1 ) = {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P(E 2 ) = {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex} Let A denotes that a person has heart attack, then P (A) = 40% = 0.40 Yoga and meditation reduces heart attack by 30. {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} Inspite of getting yoga and meditation treatment heart risk is 70% of 0.40 {tex} \Rightarrow P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex} = 0.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.70 = 0.28 Also, Drug prescription reduces the heart attack rick by 25% Even after adopting the drug prescription hear rick is 75% of 0.40 {tex} \Rightarrow P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) {/tex} = 0.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.75 = 0.30 {tex}P\left( {{E_1}|A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times 0.28}}{{\frac{1}{2} \times 0.28 + \frac{1}{2} \times 0.30}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{0.28}}{{0.28 + 0.30}} = \frac{{0.28}}{{0.58}} = \frac{{28}}{{58}} = \frac{{14}}{{29}}{/tex}
solution_html:
A patient has options to have the treatment of yoga and meditation and that of prescription of drugs.
Let these events be denoted by E1 and E2 i.e.,
E1 = Treatment of yoga and meditation
E2 = Treatment of prescription of certain drugs
P (E1) = {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex} and P(E2) = {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Let A denotes that a person has heart attack, then P (A) = 40% = 0.40
Yoga and meditation reduces heart attack by 30.
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} Inspite of getting yoga and meditation treatment heart risk is 70% of 0.40
{tex} \Rightarrow P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right){/tex}  = 0.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.70 = 0.28
Also, Drug prescription reduces the heart attack rick by 25%
Even after adopting the drug prescription hear rick is 75% of 0.40
{tex} \Rightarrow P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right) {/tex} = 0.40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.75 = 0.30
{tex}P\left( {{E_1}|A} \right) = \frac{{P({E_1})P(A|{E_1})}}{{P({E_1})P\left( {A|{E_1}} \right) + P({E_2})P\left( {A|{E_2}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times 0.28}}{{\frac{1}{2} \times 0.28 + \frac{1}{2} \times 0.30}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{{0.28}}{{0.28 + 0.30}} = \frac{{0.28}}{{0.58}} = \frac{{28}}{{58}} = \frac{{14}}{{29}}{/tex}

question_text: If each element of a second order determinant is either zero or one, what is the probability that the value of the determinant is positive? question_html: If each element of a second order determinant is either zero or one, what is the probability that the value of the determinant is positive? (Assume that the individual entries of the determinant are chosen independently, each value being assumed with probability {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}).
solution_text: There are four entries in a determinant of {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} order. Each entry may be filled up in two ways with 0 or 1. {tex}\therefore {/tex} Number of determinants that can be formed = 2 4 = 16 The value of determinants is positive in the following cases: {tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right|,\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right|,\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right| = 3{/tex} Therefore, the probability that the determinant is positive {tex} = \frac{3}{{16}}{/tex}
solution_html:
There are four entries in a determinant of {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} order. Each entry may be filled up in two ways with 0 or 1.
{tex}\therefore {/tex} Number of determinants that can be formed = 24 = 16
The value of determinants is positive in the following cases:
{tex}\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right|,\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 1&1 \end{array}} \right|,\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&1 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right| = 3{/tex}
Therefore, the probability that the determinant is positive {tex} = \frac{3}{{16}}{/tex}

question_text: An electronic assembly consists of two subsystems, say, A and B. From previous testing procedures, the following probabilities are assumed to be known: P(A fails) = 0.2 P(B fails alone) = 0.15 P(A and B fail) = 0.15 Evaluate the following probabilities P(A fails|B has failed).
question_html:
An electronic assembly consists of two subsystems, say, A and B. From previous testing procedures, the following probabilities are assumed to be known:
P(A fails) = 0.2
P(B fails alone) = 0.15
P(A and B fail) = 0.15
Evaluate the following probabilities P(A fails|B has failed).
solution_text: Let's define events; E A : A fails E B : B fails Given that: Event failed by A, P(E A ) = 0.2 Event failed by both, {tex}P\left(E_{A} \cap E_{B}\right){/tex} = 0.15 And, event failed by B alone = {tex}P\left(E_{B}\right)-P\left(E_{A} \cap E_{B}\right){/tex} 0.15 = P (E B ) - 0.15 {tex}\therefore{/tex} P (E B ) = 0.30 Therefore, {tex}P\left(E_{A} | E_{B}\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{A} \cap E_{B}\right)}{P\left(E_{B}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{0.15}{0.3}{/tex} = 0.5 Which is the required solution.
solution_html:
Let's define events;
EA : A fails
EB : B fails
Given that:
Event failed by A, P(EA) = 0.2
Event failed by both, {tex}P\left(E_{A} \cap E_{B}\right){/tex} = 0.15 
And, event failed by B alone = {tex}P\left(E_{B}\right)-P\left(E_{A} \cap E_{B}\right){/tex} 
0.15 = P (EB) - 0.15
{tex}\therefore{/tex} P (EB) = 0.30
Therefore, {tex}P\left(E_{A} | E_{B}\right)=\frac{P\left(E_{A} \cap E_{B}\right)}{P\left(E_{B}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{0.15}{0.3}{/tex}
= 0.5
Which is the required solution.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: An electronic assembly consists of two subsystems, say, A and B. From previous testing procedures, the following probabilities are assumed to be known: P(A fails) = 0.2 P(B fails alone) = 0.15 P(A and B fail) = 0.15 Evaluate the following probabilities P(A fails alone).
question_html:
An electronic assembly consists of two subsystems, say, A and B. From previous testing procedures, the following probabilities are assumed to be known:
P(A fails) = 0.2
P(B fails alone) = 0.15
P(A and B fail) = 0.15
Evaluate the following probabilities P(A fails alone).
solution_text: Let us define events; A : A fails . and B : B fails. Given: P (A) = 0.2 Event failed by both, {tex}P\left(A \cap B\right){/tex} = 0.15 We have, P(A fails alone) = P(A) - {tex}P\left(A \cap B\right){/tex} = 0.2 - 0.15 = 0.05
solution_html:
Let us define events;
A : A fails. and B : B fails.
Given: P (A) = 0.2

Event failed by both, {tex}P\left(A \cap B\right){/tex} = 0.15
We have,
P(A fails alone) = P(A) - {tex}P\left(A \cap B\right){/tex}
= 0.2 - 0.15
= 0.05

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 5 black balls. One ball is transferred from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be red in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is black.
question_html: Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls and Bag II contains 4 red and 5 black balls. One ball is transferred from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn from Bag II. The ball so drawn is found to be red in colour. Find the probability that the transferred ball is black.
solution_text: Let us define events, A 1 : Red ball is transferred from bag I to II. A 2 : Black ball is transferred from bag I to II. {tex}\therefore P\left(A_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{7}{/tex} Let X be the event that the drawn ball is red {tex}\therefore{/tex} when red ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{1}\right)=\frac{5}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} And, when black ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex} Hence, {tex}P\left(A_{2} | X\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(X | A_{1}\right)+P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{16}{31}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let us define events,
A1 : Red ball is transferred from bag I to II.
A2 : Black ball is transferred from bag I to II.
{tex}\therefore P\left(A_{1}\right)=\frac{3}{7}{/tex} and {tex}P\left(A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{7}{/tex} 
Let X be the event that the drawn ball is red
{tex}\therefore{/tex} when red ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{1}\right)=\frac{5}{10}{/tex}  = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
And, when black ball is transferred from bag I to II, {tex}P\left(X | A_{2}\right)=\frac{4}{10}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}P\left(A_{2} | X\right)=\frac{P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{P\left(A_{1}\right) P\left(X | A_{1}\right)+P\left(A_{2}\right) P\left(X | A_{2}\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{\frac{3}{7} \times \frac{1}{2}+\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{2}{5}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{16}{31}{/tex}
